title: XSS Reflection Scanner
description: A tool that crawls target endpoints for input parameters, injects common XSS payloads (script tags, event handlers, SVG injections), and detects which inputs are reflected in the response without sanitization. Demo server has endpoints that reflect input raw, escape HTML entities, and use CSP headers respectively.
summary: Built an XSS reflection scanner with an Express demo server exposing three endpoints at different security levels (raw, HTML-escaped, CSP-protected). All 16 node:test tests passed, covering true positives, false positives, output format, and edge cases.
concepts_demonstrated: Reflected cross-site scripting (XSS)
HTML entity encoding as XSS mitigation
Content-Security-Policy as secondary defence
XSS payload injection and detection
Severity classification (HIGH vs MEDIUM)

title: Port Scanner with Service Fingerprinting
description: Scans a range of localhost ports, identifies which are open, and attempts to fingerprint the service by analysing banner responses. Demo server opens three ports with different services: HTTP (Express), raw TCP echo, and a fake SMTP relay.
summary: Built a parallel TCP port scanner that fingerprints services via banner analysis (HTTP response headers, SMTP 220 greeting, echo reflection). Demo server exposes HTTP, TCP echo, and fake SMTP on consecutive ports. All 22 node:test tests passed on first run.
concepts_demonstrated: TCP port scanning
Service banner grabbing and fingerprinting
HTTP, SMTP, and echo service identification
Parallel async TCP probing with Promise.all
Information leakage via server banners

title: SQL Injection Detector
description: Sends parameterized and unparameterized queries to endpoints backed by SQLite. Detects error-based, union-based, and boolean-based blind SQLi. Demo server has a login form and search endpoint — one using raw string concatenation, one using parameterized queries.
summary: Built a SQL injection detector with an Express + node:sqlite demo server exposing vulnerable (string-concatenated) and safe (parameterized) login and search endpoints. All 20 node:test tests passed, covering error-based SQLi, authentication bypass, and UNION-based data exfiltration.
concepts_demonstrated: Error-based SQL injection via unbalanced quotes
Authentication bypass with always-true SQL condition
UNION-based SQL injection for data exfiltration
Parameterized queries as the complete mitigation
SQL error information disclosure as a secondary vulnerability

title: Hash Collision Demonstrator
description: Compares MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 by hashing large sets of inputs and measuring collision resistance. Demonstrates known MD5 weaknesses. Demo server provides a hash-verification API that the tool tests against.
summary: Built a hash algorithm security scanner with an Express demo server exposing MD5 (vulnerable), SHA-1 (deprecated), and SHA-256 (safe) hash endpoints. The server computes a live birthday-attack collision on a 32-bit MD5 prefix at startup. All 23 node:test tests passed.
concepts_demonstrated: MD5 cryptographic weakness and practical collision attacks
SHA-1 deprecation via the SHAttered attack (2017)
Birthday paradox and its impact on hash collision resistance
SHA-256 as the current minimum secure standard
Algorithm agility and deprecation lifecycle

title: Brute Force Rate Limiter Demo
description: Tests login endpoints for brute force protection. Measures how many attempts before lockout, checks if lockout is IP-based or account-based, tests if rate limits reset properly. Demo server has a login route with configurable rate limiting.
summary: Built a brute force protection demo with an Express server exposing a vulnerable endpoint (no rate limiting) and a protected endpoint (IP-based lockout after 5 consecutive failures with 30-second window). A scanner tool probes both and reports findings as structured JSON. All 20 node:test tests passed on first run.
concepts_demonstrated: Brute force login attacks and credential guessing
IP-based rate limiting and account lockout
Fixed-window lockout with configurable threshold
Retry-After header signalling for 429 responses
True positive detection vs. false positive suppression in security scanners

title: File Entropy Analyzer
description: Calculates Shannon entropy of files to detect packed, encrypted, or compressed content. High entropy suggests obfuscation. Demo server serves files with varying entropy levels. Tool analyzes each and classifies them.
summary: Built a Shannon entropy scanner with an Express demo server serving four files at different entropy levels (0, ~4, ~8, ~8 bits/byte). The tool fetches, analyzes, and classifies each file, flagging the two high-entropy blobs as encrypted_or_packed. All 22 node:test tests passed on the first run.
concepts_demonstrated: Shannon entropy as a randomness metric for byte streams
High entropy (>7.5 b/B) as an indicator of encryption or packing
False-positive suppression: plain text and structured data are not flagged
File content classification: plain_text / natural_language / compressed / encrypted_or_packed
Safe endpoint restricting binary content delivery vs. unrestricted download

title: CSRF Token Validator
description: Tests form endpoints for CSRF protection. Checks for token presence, token rotation on each request, SameSite cookie attributes, and referer header validation. Demo server has a funds transfer form — one protected, one unprotected.
summary: Built a CSRF token validator with an Express demo server exposing a vulnerable funds-transfer endpoint (no token check), a protected endpoint (synchronizer token + single-use rotation), and cookie endpoints with and without SameSite=Strict. All 21 node:test tests passed on the first run.
concepts_demonstrated: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attack mechanism
Synchronizer token pattern as the primary CSRF defence
Single-use token rotation to prevent token replay attacks
SameSite=Strict cookie attribute as a complementary browser-level defence
Session-scoped token isolation (session A tokens cannot be used by session B)

title: AES Encryption Mode Comparison
description: Encrypts identical plaintext with AES in ECB, CBC, CTR, and GCM modes. Visually demonstrates why ECB is insecure (pattern preservation). Demo server has encrypt/decrypt endpoints for each mode.
summary: Built an AES mode comparison tool with an Express demo server exposing ECB (vulnerable), CBC (legacy), CTR (unauthenticated), and GCM (recommended) encrypt endpoints. A scanner detects determinism in ECB, missing auth tags in CBC/CTR, and confirms GCM is clean. All 22 node:test tests passed on the first run.
concepts_demonstrated: ECB mode determinism — identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks, leaking data structure
CBC padding oracle vulnerability — unauthenticated mode susceptible to bit-flipping and padding attacks
CTR mode as unauthenticated encryption — no integrity protection despite a random IV
AES-GCM as authenticated encryption — combines confidentiality and integrity in one pass
IV uniqueness requirements — IV reuse under CTR/GCM enables plaintext XOR recovery

title: Input Sanitization Library
description: A comprehensive input sanitization module that handles HTML escaping, SQL parameterization, path normalization, and command injection prevention. Demo server has endpoints that use the library and endpoints that don't. Tests prove the difference.
summary: Built an input sanitization library with an Express demo server exposing three vulnerable/safe endpoint pairs (HTML escaping, path normalization, command injection). The scanner tool sends attack payloads to all six endpoints and reports findings as structured JSON. All 20 node:test tests passed on the first run.
concepts_demonstrated: HTML entity encoding to prevent cross-site scripting (XSS)
Path normalization using path.resolve + startsWith to prevent directory traversal
Shell metacharacter rejection to prevent command injection
Sanitize-at-output vs sanitize-at-input — applying the right layer for each vector
True positive detection and false positive suppression in a security scanner

title: Log File Anomaly Detector
description: Parses server access logs and detects anomalies: SQL injection attempts in URLs, directory traversal sequences, suspicious scanner user agents, request-rate spikes via sliding-window analysis, and consecutive server error bursts. Demo server pre-seeds logs with all five anomaly types alongside clean baseline traffic.
summary: Built a rule-based log anomaly detector with an Express demo server that seeds realistic access logs containing five injected attack patterns. The tool fetches logs over HTTP, applies five detection rules, and emits structured JSON findings with HIGH/MEDIUM severity ratings. All 23 node:test tests passed on the first run.
concepts_demonstrated: URL-decode-before-match to catch %2e%2e and other encoded traversal evasions
Sliding-window rate analysis to detect brute-force login bursts
Consecutive 5xx error tracking as an error-spike indicator
Known scanner user-agent fingerprinting (sqlmap, Nikto, masscan, WPScan)
True positive vs false positive separation in rule-based detection

title: JWT Vulnerability Scanner
description: Tests JWT tokens for common vulnerabilities: none algorithm acceptance, weak HMAC secrets, algorithm confusion (RS256 to HS256), expired token acceptance, missing claims validation. Demo server has a protected API using JWT auth with intentional flaws.
summary: Built a JWT vulnerability scanner with a FastAPI demo server exposing three attack surfaces: alg=none acceptance, HS256 with a weak crackable secret, and missing expiry validation. The scanner probes all three, cracks the secret via dictionary attack, forges an expired token, and verifies the safe endpoint rejects unsigned tokens. All 27 pytest tests passed on first run after fixing an httpx edge case with trailing-space header values.
concepts_demonstrated: JWT none-algorithm attack — unsigned tokens accepted when alg=none is not explicitly rejected
Weak HS256 secret cracking via offline dictionary attack against the HMAC-SHA256 signature
Expired JWT acceptance — missing exp claim validation allows indefinitely valid stolen tokens
Constant-time comparison (hmac.compare_digest) to prevent timing side-channels in secret cracking detection
Strict algorithm allowlist as the correct mitigation for alg=none and algorithm confusion
